question: [File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/retention/RetentionJob.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: prune()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the prune() job deletes entity stake history (via EntityStakeHistoryRepository.prune()) without the staking account owner's consent, removing the historical record of staking authorizations that the account holder never approved for deletion?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/retention/RetentionJob.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: prune()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the prune() job deletes registered node history (via RegisteredNodeHistoryRepository.prune()) without the node operator's consent, removing authorization records for node registration transactions that the operator never approved for deletion?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/retention/RetentionJob.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: prune()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the prune() job deletes hook history (via HookHistoryRepository.prune()) without the hook owner's consent, removing records of hook authorization transactions that the owner never approved for deletion?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/retention/RetentionJob.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: prune()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the prune() job deletes reconciliation job records (via ReconciliationJobRepository.prune()) without any authorization from the accounts whose balances were reconciled, removing audit evidence of balance authorization without account owner consent?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/retention/RetentionJob.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: prune()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the prune() job deletes contract log records (via ContractLogRepository.prune()) without the contract owner's consent, removing event emission records that serve as proof of authorized contract state changes?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/retention/RetentionJob.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: prune()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the prune() job deletes token history records (via TokenHistoryRepository.prune()) without the token treasury's consent, removing the historical record of token configuration authorizations that the treasury account never approved for deletion?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/retention/RetentionJob.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: prune()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the prune() job deletes contract transaction records (via ContractTransactionRepository.prune()) without the contract caller's consent, removing proof that a specific account authorized a specific contract invocation at a specific time?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/retention/RetentionJob.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: prune()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the prune() job has no mechanism to check whether a TransactionSignature record is still referenced by an active dispute or legal proceeding, causing deletion of authorization evidence without the signer's consent and potentially enabling repudiation attacks?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/retention/RetentionJob.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: prune()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the prune() job's only authorization check is retentionProperties.isEnabled(), which is a global flag with no per-user or per-transaction granularity, meaning enabling retention authorizes deletion of ALL users' transaction records without individual signer consent?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/retention/RetentionJob.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: prune()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the prune() job uses a single endTimestamp derived from findLatestWithOffset() to delete records across ALL transaction types simultaneously, meaning a single timestamp calculation without per-owner authorization gates the deletion of every signer's records across all tables?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/retention/RetentionJob.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: RecordFileIterator.hasNext()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the initialization logic in hasNext() where findNextBetween(0, max.getConsensusEnd()) is called with a hardcoded lower bound of 0, causing the iterator to always start from the very first record file and prune the oldest transaction signatures without those original signers' consent?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/retention/RetentionJob.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: prune()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the prune() job logs the count of pruned records but never logs which specific transaction IDs or signer accounts were affected, making it impossible for signers to detect that their authorization records were deleted without consent?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/retention/RetentionJob.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: prune()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the prune() job's exception handler at line 61-63 only logs errors and does not roll back already-committed prune transactions, meaning partial deletion of transaction signatures can occur without the affected signers' consent even when the job encounters errors?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/retention/RetentionJob.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: prune()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the prune() job processes all retentionRepositories in a forEach loop without any per-repository authorization check, meaning a single job execution can delete transaction signatures, contract results, token transfers, and staking records all without any of the respective owners' consent?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/retention/RetentionJob.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: prune()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the prune() job uses counters.merge(table, count, Long::sum) to accumulate deletion counts but never checks whether the deleted records belonged to consenting owners, making the job's success metric (rows deleted) completely decoupled from authorization compliance?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/retention/RetentionJob.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: prune()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the prune() job's frequency is controlled by retentionProperties.getFrequency() with no minimum floor enforced in code, so if frequency is set to a very short interval, transaction signatures are deleted at a rate that prevents signers from even detecting the unauthorized deletion before it occurs?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/retention/RetentionJob.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: prune()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the prune() job deletes TransactionSignature records (via TransactionSignatureRepository.prune()) which are the cryptographic proof that a transaction was authorized by its required signers, and once deleted, there is no way to reconstruct whether a transaction was properly authorized, enabling retroactive repudiation?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/retention/RetentionJob.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: prune()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the prune() job has no concept of transaction type sensitivity, treating a simple transfer's signature records identically to a multi-sig treasury transaction's signature records, deleting both without the respective signers' consent based solely on timestamp?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/retention/RetentionJob.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: prune()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the prune() job's retentionPeriod is read from retentionProperties.getPeriod() at job execution time with no validation that the period is long enough to preserve legally required authorization records, allowing deletion of transaction signatures before regulatory retention requirements are met without signer consent?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/retention/RetentionJob.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: prune()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the prune() job's shouldPrune(table) check uses string matching on table names derived from class names, and if a new RetentionRepository implementation is added whose class name does not end in 'Repository', getTableName() produces an unexpected table name that bypasses the exclude list, causing unauthorized deletion of that table's transaction records?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/retention/RetentionJob.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: getTableName()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the getTableName() method's use of Utility.toSnakeCase() on a class name to produce a table name that collides with an unintended table in the include set, causing shouldPrune() to return true for a table whose records' owners never consented to deletion?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/retention/RetentionJob.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: prune()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the prune() job deletes records from ALL tables in a single scheduled execution without any per-table owner notification or consent mechanism, meaning a token holder, contract owner, and transaction signer all lose their records simultaneously without any of them being consulted?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/retention/RetentionJob.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: prune()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the prune() job's endTimestamp is derived from a single record file's consensusEnd value, and by submitting transactions that create record files with manipulated consensusEnd values, cause the job to use an attacker-influenced timestamp to delete other users' transaction signatures without their consent?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/retention/RetentionJob.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: prune()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the prune() job has no idempotency key or deletion receipt mechanism, meaning once transaction signatures are deleted without owner consent, there is no cryptographic proof that the deletion was unauthorized, enabling the attacker to deny the deletion ever occurred?

[File: 'File Name: importer/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/importer/retention/RetentionJob.java -> Scope: High: Authorizing transactions without approval from signers/owners'] [Function: prune()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the prune() job's transactionOperations.executeWithoutResult() does not check the Spring Security context for any authorization token before executing bulk deletes, confirming that no signer or owner identity is verified at the database transaction level before their records are deleted?
